caveats: a: The occupation table is for broad NAICS 523000 and includes business models absent from 523910.
a: Employment shares are not wage-weighted task shares and exclude self-employed workers.
a: The very small lens-eligible subset may have a materially different staffing mix from the legal NAICS population.

rho: Exploration or vendor procurement does not establish production use or avoided labor.
rho: Rare transactions and changing market regimes limit reliable automation of investment judgment.

e: Venture-capital management companies may instead belong in portfolio management, while investment vehicles may belong in fund codes.
e: The margin-bridged dataset count can include legal entities whose receipts and capital gains do not correspond to service EBITDA.

s5: The source has no 523910, deal-size, or control-rights cut.
s5: A sale of investments or contractual rights is not a sale of the operating intermediary.

q: The party receiving the efficiency benefit may be a fund, manager, limited partner, employee, or counterparty depending on structure.
d5: The BLS projection covers all of NAICS 523000 and is not a six-digit forecast.
d5: Real output may not track constant-quality demand for principal investment services.
d5: Transaction volume and investment returns are highly cyclical and sensitive to financing markets.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The NAICS core acts as principal and puts its own capital at risk, earning realized or unrealized investment gains and spreads rather than a conventional recurring service fee. The small lens-eligible subset repeatedly originates and exits contracts with external counterparties, but its economics depend on deal selection, purchase price, holding period, financing, and exit proceeds.
lens.excluded_activities: Investment clubs, individual or passive own-account vehicles, funds or holding entities without transferable operating services, captive affiliates, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and businesses whose primary activity is brokerage, investment banking, portfolio management for others, investment advice, or custody.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market operating firms classified in 523910 that repeatedly source, analyze, acquire, manage, and sell financial contracts or contractual rights as principals to external counterparties and have a transferable team, process, data, and control structure.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment counts for broad NAICS 523000 rather than wage-weighted tasks in 523910. I map research, screening, financial modeling, document review, diligence synthesis, reporting, monitoring, compliance support, and administrative work to AI-exposed tasks, then discount relationship sourcing, negotiation, investment judgment, governance, and accountable capital decisions. The broad sector includes large broker-dealers and advisers, so its 39.70% business-and-financial, 19.18% office-support, 17.33% sales, and 15.29% management mix may overstate sales and operations staffing while understating principal-investment judgment in 523910.
caveats: The occupation table is for broad NAICS 523000 and includes business models absent from 523910.
Employment shares are not wage-weighted task shares and exclude self-employed workers.
The very small lens-eligible subset may have a materially different staffing mix from the legal NAICS population.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit payroll-weighted task study of venture-capital companies, tax-lien principals, royalty and lease dealers, and other 523910 operators, separating sourcing, underwriting, diligence, modeling, legal review, monitoring, administration, investment committee work, and already-automated activity.
a.rationale: The broader securities and investment sector is dominated by cognitive, document-heavy, quantitative, sales, and administrative occupations. FINRA identifies AI uses in customer communications, portfolio and trading processes, compliance, risk management, and administration. Those tools can compress analyst and operations hours, but differentiated sourcing, valuation under uncertainty, negotiation, approval, and capital accountability remain human-led.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for broad NAICS 523000 from $721.7 billion in 2024 to $892.4 billion in 2034, a 2.1% annual increase, rather than constant-quality demand for the narrow lens-eligible 523910 basket. I compound approximately that broad rate over five years for the base and widen for capital-market cycles, financing conditions, valuation changes, tax policy, commodity and royalty cycles, and the small eligible population. The broad sector may overstate stability because fee-based advisory and brokerage activity diversify the highly cyclical principal-investing component.
caveats: The BLS projection covers all of NAICS 523000 and is not a six-digit forecast.
Real output may not track constant-quality demand for principal investment services.
Transaction volume and investment returns are highly cyclical and sensitive to financing markets.
d5.change_evidence: A six-digit constant-price transaction-volume and spread index for 523910, split among venture capital, tax liens, mineral or royalty rights, clubs, and other principals, with deal counts, deployed capital, exits, write-offs, and forecast assumptions.
d5.rationale: Broad securities and financial-investment real output is projected to grow, supporting a modestly larger five-year activity pool. Demand for principal intermediation remains cyclical, and the examples in 523910 span venture capital, tax liens, investment clubs, and mineral rights, so a contraction case remains credible.

caveats: The NAICS definition conflicts with the screen's recurring outsourced-service requirement for most establishments.
Venture-capital management companies may instead belong in portfolio management, while investment vehicles may belong in fund codes.
The margin-bridged dataset count can include legal entities whose receipts and capital gains do not correspond to service EBITDA.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level classification of the frozen dataset-band population using legal-entity type, audited revenue composition, employee count, management-company agreements, beneficial ownership, repeat transaction history, and whether transferable operating services exist apart from invested capital.
e.rationale: Census defines 523910 around principals buying or selling financial contracts for their own account and lists investment clubs, venture-capital companies, tax-lien dealers, and mineral-rights or lease dealers. That core usually represents principal capital activity rather than a recurring outsourced service supplied to customers. Only a small subset with a separable operating team, repeat external-counterparty process, normalized operating economics, and transferable control fits the frozen lens; passive vehicles, clubs, and non-control financing entities do not.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 523910 ($9,520,051,539) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 523910 ($46,292,055,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1165 (margins.json[523]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Investments & Asset Management EBITDA/Sales 11.65%); revenue/employee $790,425 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 523910); class <5: 9,038 firms, avg revenue/firm $820,990 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $95,645 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 968 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,790,246 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $441,564 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 514 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,378,568 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $976,103 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 460 firms, avg revenue/firm $27,160,726 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,164,225 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 161 firms, avg revenue/firm $56,438,845 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,575,125 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 113 firms, avg revenue/firm $82,440,832 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,604,357 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 734

caveats: No source measures software substitution for the six-digit industry.
The operator concept is unusually weak where the legal entity is primarily a pool of capital.
Regulation, contracting, and capital authority may preserve a legal operator without preserving much labor.
o.change_evidence: A longitudinal cohort of current 523910 workflows and transactions classifying year-five activity as human-operated, human-supervised software, fully automated or self-served, migrated to another NAICS, or eliminated, with capital authority and liability mapped for each category.
o.rationale: AI can automate screening, information retrieval, modeling, monitoring, document synthesis, and some execution, while self-directed platforms can eliminate portions of club and small-principal activity. A continuing operator is still required to own capital, set mandates, negotiate, approve investments, contract with counterparties, manage conflicts, oversee models, and bear legal and economic consequences. The operator-required share is lower than for customized advice because much of the core activity is already digital and own-account.

caveats: No source directly measures retention of AI-derived benefit in 523910.
Investment returns and spreads are volatile and should not be confused with recurring service margins.
The party receiving the efficiency benefit may be a fund, manager, limited partner, employee, or counterparty depending on structure.
q.change_evidence: Firm-level pre/post automation data linking software and review costs, labor hours, bid prices, spreads, realized investment gains and losses, portfolio-company support, partner compensation, and any investor or counterparty sharing over complete deal cycles.
q.rationale: Principal investors do not generally bill customers for labor hours: implemented research and operating savings initially remain inside deal economics. Retention then leaks through more competitive bids, faster information diffusion, vendor costs, higher diligence volume, partner or employee compensation, investor sharing where a separate manager exists, and losses from model error. Because the screen applies only to a small service-like operating subset, there is no stable contractual pass-through benchmark.

rho.bridge: FINRA observes cautious adoption of third-party generative-AI tools across regulated financial firms, not five-year implementation of exposed wages by LMM 523910 principals. I translate current internal summarization, cross-dataset analysis, policy retrieval, and longer-standing investment-process uses into a five-year implementation range, then discount model error, sparse deal data, regime change, cybersecurity, confidential information, vendor oversight, explainability, legal review, and approval controls. Broker-dealer regulation is not identical to 523910, but the stated control risks likely overstate formal FINRA burdens for some principals and understate bespoke valuation and capital-loss risks.
caveats: FINRA evidence centers on broker-dealers and other regulated financial firms rather than 523910 principals.
Exploration or vendor procurement does not establish production use or avoided labor.
Rare transactions and changing market regimes limit reliable automation of investment judgment.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year panel of LMM 523910 firms measuring workflow-level deployment, baseline and post-deployment hours, investment error rates, overrides, realized losses, data incidents, vendor costs, review time, and avoided hiring.
rho.rationale: FINRA reports that financial firms are proceeding cautiously with generative AI and are generally using vendor-supported tools for internal summaries, data analysis, and policy retrieval. It also documents AI applications in portfolio management, trading, compliance, risk, and administration. Implementation should therefore be meaningful but bounded by the high consequence of erroneous investment decisions and by the need to protect proprietary and counterparty data.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or transfer plans among U.S. employer-business owners across industries, not completed qualifying control transfers among lens-eligible 523910 firms. I center on its 22% intention rate, widen for active financial-firm consolidation and owner concentration, and discount failed plans, portfolio-asset sales, fund restructurings, partner admissions, investment exits, and internal changes that do not transfer the operating company. The broad survey may overstate completed transfers but may understate sponsor-driven sales of mature operating platforms.
caveats: Owner intentions do not equal completed transfers.
The source has no 523910, deal-size, or control-rights cut.
A sale of investments or contractual rights is not a sale of the operating intermediary.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from PitchBook, Capital IQ, Form D and adviser filings, state registrations, purchase agreements, and beneficial-ownership changes that distinguishes operating-company control sales from portfolio exits, fund closings, partner changes, and asset-only transactions and matches the frozen EBITDA band.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or take public their business within five years. For 523910, many visible transactions concern underlying investments rather than control of the intermediary itself, so the base does not treat ordinary asset exits or fund changes as qualifying firm transfers.

principal_driver: Document-heavy sourcing, diligence, modeling, monitoring, and administration offer meaningful workflow compression where a real operating platform exists.
principal_weakness: Most of the NAICS population acts for its own account and may be a capital vehicle rather than a recurring external service business with transferable operating EBITDA.
